coredata.dc:title: Rapid and high yield synthesis of carbon dots with chelating ability derived from acrylamide/chitosan for selective detection of ferrous ions
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guanxiong
Li, Baoqiang
Liu, Ying
Feng, Yujie
Jia, Dechang
Zhou, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and high efficient synthesis of carbon dots is crucial problem for scalable synthesis and practical biomedical or environmental application. However, synthesis of carbon dots still has the challenges, such as long reaction time of hydrothermal carbonization and low synthetic yield of rapid microwave carbonization. To develop rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots, we proposed acrylamide/chitosan boost the synthetic yield of carbon dots via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. The reaction time and synthesis yield were 30 min and 45.9%, respectively. As increasing of the carbon-carbon double bonds content, the synthetic yield of carbon dots (45.9%) was improved 5.85 and 4.53 times higher than carbon dots derived from chitosan or acrylamide, respectively. Carbon dots with amino and carboxylic groups exhibited the chelating ability benefiting for detection of ferrous ions, and the detection limit for ferrous ions was as low as 160 nM with the concentration range of 0–50 μM. Rapid and high yield synthesis carbon dots derived from acrylamide/chitosan provides carbonaceous probe with chelating ability for detection of ferrous ions sensitively and specifically.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave-hydrothermal carbonization
High synthetic yield
Ferrous ions detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots derived from acrylamide/chitosan via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization
Florescent and surface properties of CDs-NH2
Sensitive detection of Fe2+ based on carbon dots with chelating ability
Rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots derived from precursor containing carbon-carbon double bonds
Optical and surface properties of carbon dots with amino groups and carboxylic group
Selectivity and sensitivity of Fe2+ detection using CDs-NH2 as fluorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Rapid and high synthetic yield carbon dots derived from chitosan and precursor containing carbon-carbon double bonds via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. (A) Schematic presentation of carbon-carbon double bonds boosting synthetic yield of CDs-NH2. (B) Synthetic yield of CDs-NH2 derived from different CC content. (C) Synthetic yield of CDs derived from different precursors containing CC (including AE, BA, AA and AAM). (D) Synthetic yield of carbon dots derived from different precursors via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization.

Optical and surface properties of CDs-NH2 and CDs-COOH. (A) The excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of CDs-NH2 under different excitation wavelengths. (B) The excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of CDs with carboxyl group under different excitation wavelengths. (C) FTIR of CDs-NH2 and (D) CDs with carboxyl group. (E) Fluorescence intensity of CDs-NH2 varying with pH. (F) Fluorescence intensity of CDs-COOH varying with pH. (G) XRD pattern of CDs-NH2. (H) XRD pattern of CDs-COOH.

Chelating ability and XPS spectrum of CDs-NH2. (A) Effect of EDTA on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-NH2/Fe2+ complex with different concentrations of Fe2+. (B) N1s high resolution peak-splitting fitting of CDs-NH2. (C) C1s high resolution peak-splitting fitting of CDs-NH2. (D) O1s high resolution peak-splitting fitting of CDs-NH2.

Ferrous ions detection of CDs-NH2 with chelating ability. (A) Selective quenching effect of CDs-NH2 against with various metal ions. (B) The relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of CDs-NH2 in the presence of interfering ions (300 μM), and following treatment of the solution with 300 μM Fe2+. (C) Representative PL emission with increasing Fe2+ concentrations (0–300 μM). (D) The dependence of (F0/F)-1 on concentrations of Fe2+, the inset displays photographs with Fe2+ concentrations of 0 and 300 μΜ. (E) Ferrous ions detection based on chelating ability.
The synthetic yield of CDs derived from different precursors via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization.
Detection limit of CDs for ferrous ions in literatures and this work.

head.ce:title: Rapid and high yield synthesis of carbon dots with chelating ability derived from acrylamide/chitosan for selective detection of ferrous ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Ceramics, State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Ceramics, State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MIIT Key Laboratory of Advanced Structural-Functional Integration Materials & Green Manufacturing Technology, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MIIT Key Laboratory of Advanced Structural-Functional Integration Materials & Green Manufacturing Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Advanced Ceramics, State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and high efficient synthesis of carbon dots is crucial problem for scalable synthesis and practical biomedical or environmental application. However, synthesis of carbon dots still has the challenges, such as long reaction time of hydrothermal carbonization and low synthetic yield of rapid microwave carbonization. To develop rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots, we proposed acrylamide/chitosan boost the synthetic yield of carbon dots via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. The reaction time and synthesis yield were 30 min and 45.9%, respectively. As increasing of the carbon-carbon double bonds content, the synthetic yield of carbon dots (45.9%) was improved 5.85 and 4.53 times higher than carbon dots derived from chitosan or acrylamide, respectively. Carbon dots with amino and carboxylic groups exhibited the chelating ability benefiting for detection of ferrous ions, and the detection limit for ferrous ions was as low as 160 nM with the concentration range of 0–50 μM. Rapid and high yield synthesis carbon dots derived from acrylamide/chitosan provides carbonaceous probe with chelating ability for detection of ferrous ions sensitively and specifically.

ce:section.ce:para: Various types of heavy metal ions have received extensive attention. Among them, iron element plays an essential role in environmental as well as many biological processes [1–4]. The electron transformation between Fe2+ and Fe3+ occurs in physiological process and biochemical reactions, such as cellular metabolism, enzymatic reactions and redox reactions [5,6]. Although iron element is a necessary trace element for human body, the high level of iron ions content in body will lead to chronic or acute iron poisoning [4,7–10]. Iron deficiency and overload can interfere with cellular homeostasis and induce various biological disorders, so iron intake is very important. The iron intake in the body is mainly derived from food and drinking water. The iron ion content is an important indicator for evaluating water quality. Therefore, it is particularly important to study a cheap, efficient, simple, sensitive and selective method for detecting iron ion. Currently, many methods are available for the detection of Fe2+ [11]. Among them, fluorescence technique was used to detect metal ions due to its simplicity, sensitivity, low-cost, instantaneous response and capable of specific recognition of ferrous ions [12–14].

Nanomaterials are used in ionic or molecular detection, and carbon dots (CDs) detection for metal ions have been widely reported. Liu et al. [15] found that target nucleic acid sequences with a high association rate constant are beneficial to improve the lateral flow assays performance. Jin et al. [16] used nanoparticles as the theranostic agent, it permits targeted imaging and photodynamic therapy of triple negative breast cancer both in 3D tumor model and living mice. Miao et al. [17] developed a facile route to synthesize carbon dots with red emission which was an excellent fluorescent bio-imaging unit for theranostic agent. Li et al. [18] developed a pen-on-paper strategy based on two custom-made pens to fully write paper-based microfluidic biosensors through directly writing both microfluidic channels and electrodes. As a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, CDs have good luminescent properties, small size characteristics and also have excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence stability. Therefore, it has a good application prospect in the fields of ion/molecule detection [19–22]. At present, hydrothermal synthesis has a long time to synthesize CDs and a low yield because the precursor selection does not contain unsaturated double bonds [14,23]. The yield of carbon dots synthesized by microwave method is low, the synthetic yield of CDs derived from chitosan is 6.4%within 9.5 min [24], and the synthetic yield of CDs derived from succinic acid/tris (2-aminoethyl) amine is 17.3% [25] within 5 min. At the same time, the temperature of microwave method inside is uncontrollable. The choice of microwave-hydrothermal carbonization instead of hydrothermal carbonization can shorten the reaction time for synthesis of carbon dots. Compared with microwave synthesis, microwave-hydrothermal carbonization synthesis has the advantage of controllable pressure and temperature, avoiding side reactions and improving the synthetic yield. But the synthetic yield of carbon dots is relative low (usually <10%) via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. For example, using top-down method, the synthetic yield of carbon dots derived from graphite oxide is 2.5% within 45 min [26]. For bottom-up method, Liu et al. [27] reported an approach to synthesize CDs derived from goose feathers by taking 40 min at 180 °C by microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. Zheng et al. [28] spent 120 min in synthesis CDs derived from DL-malic acid/formamide by microwave-hydrothermal carbonization at 190 °C. Guo et al. [29] reported a CDs derived from glucose/2-methyl-1,3-propanediol by taking 5 min at 120 °C via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. Zheng et al. [30] also developed organosilane-functionalized CDs derived from citric acid/N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane by taking 5 min at 180 °C via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. However, the synthetic yield of carbon dots via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization was not reported in these literatures.

Here the acrylamide and chitosan served as precursor of CDs, which also provides nitrogen source for CDs with amino groups (CDs-NH2). To develop rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots, we proposed acrylamide/chitosan boost the synthetic yield of carbon dots via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. The surface groups, chelating ability and ferrous ions detection performance were characterized and demonstrated.

Chitosan with viscosity average molecular weight of 3.4 × 105 and degree of deacetylation of 91% was purchased from Qingdao Haihui Bioengineering Co. (Qingdao, China). Acrolein (AE) was purchased from Yaohua Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China), butyl acrylate (BA) was purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China), acrylic acid (AA) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China) and acrylamide (AAM) was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Research Institute. (Tianjin, China). All solutions were prepared using deionized water from a Milli-Q water purification system with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ. Dialysis bags (retained molecular weight 1000 Da) were obtained from Solarbio (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots derived from acrylamide/chitosan via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization
ce:section.ce:para:
Rapid and synthesis of carbon dots was carried out in microwave-hydrothermal carbonization (XH-800S, Beijing XiangHu Science and Technology Development Co., LTD), in which unsaturated amides (carboxylic acids or ester) and chitosan served as precursors of CDs. The precursors were dissolved in dilute acetic acid. The mixture was sealed into a polyetheretherketone (PEEK) autoclave and microwave-hydrothermal carbonization reaction at 220 °C for 1 h. Through a 0.22 μm membrane, the upper brown suspension was filtered so as to remove large particles. Dialysis was carried out using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 500. Water was changed every two hours for 24 h. Long-term dialysis and magneton stirring were used to remove unreacted raw materials. CDs-NH2 powder was obtained by dialyzing against deionized water and lyophilization. To demonstrate that carbon‑carbon double bonds can increase the synthesis yield of CDs via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization, acrylamide severing as precursor to study the effect of different carbon-carbon double bond content (0, 14, 28, 42, 56 mmol) on the synthesis yield. To verify the universality of carbon-carbon double bonds boosting high-yield synthesis, CDs were synthesized from chitosan and different precursors containing carbon-carbon double bonds (including AE, BA, AA and AAM). Carbon dots with amino groups (CDs-NH2) and carbon dots with carboxylic acid groups (CDs-COOH) were synthesized by choosing acrylamide/chitosan and acrylic acid/chitosan, respectively. The synthetic yield was determined according to the following equation:
(1)
Synthetic yield =
M CD
M CA
× 100 %
where MCD and MCA are the mass of as-prepared CDs and total mass of precursor, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Florescent and surface properties of CDs-NH2
ce:section.ce:para:
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorometer equipped with a Xe lamp at ambient conditions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the CDs were performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with Al/K α as the source, and the energy step size was set as 0.100 eV. The crystalline structure was studied by an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. The function groups of carbon dots were characterized by FTIR (Nicolet 380, Thermo Electron Corporation, MA, USA). The quantum yield (QY) was calculated by counting the integrated PL intensities (excited at 360 nm) and the absorbance (at 360 nm) of CDs using quinine sulfate as a reference (QY 0.54 at 360 nm).Quinine sulfate (QS) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. The absorption of samples was kept below 0.05. The QY was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
∅ CDs
=
∅ QS
×
I CDs
I QS
×
A QS
A CDs
×
n CDs
n QS
2
× 100 %
where ∅ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index, and A is the absorbance. The subscript CDs and QS refers to carbon dots and quinine sulfate, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of Fe2+ based on carbon dots with chelating ability
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the competitive chelating ability of CDs-NH2 and EDTA for Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-NH2/Fe2+ with and without EDTA chelating agent were characterized. Three groups of 0.6 mg/ml 10 ml CDs-NH2 solution were prepared, then 10 ml Fe2+ solution with different concentration (100 μM, 500 μM and 1000 μM) was added respectively. FL emission spectra were recorded at 330 nm excitation wavelength. Finally, the same amount of Na2EDTA was added to the three groups of mixed solutions, and FL emission spectra were recorded again at the excitation wavelength of 330 nm after 10 min of equilibrium. The two fluorescence intensities of the three groups of mixed solutions were analyzed statistically.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid and high-yield synthesis of carbon dots derived from precursor containing carbon-carbon double bonds
ce:section.ce:para: The microwave-hydrothermal carbonization process is shown in Fig. 1 .A. To synthesize CDs rapidly, microwave-hydrothermal carbonization was used. To improve the synthesis yield of CDs, chitosan and acrylamide were selected as precursors to achieve rapid conversion within 30–60 min. The synthesis yield and function groups of CDs can be modulated by screening the precursor source [31–35]. Microwave can rapidly convert chitosan and carrots into carbon within 4–30 min [36,37]. With the increase of acrylamide in the microwave-hydrothermal carbonization, the yield of CDs-NH2 increased significantly. Acrylamide and chitosan served as carbon source to increase yield. The synthetic yield of CDs derived from single chitosan is low (6.7%) under microwave-hydrothermal carbonization, and the synthetic yield of CDs derived single acrylamide is low (8.3%) under microwave-hydrothermal carbonization as well. Wang et al. [38] pointed that when employing unsaturated amides or carboxylic acids and chitosan as two-component carbon source, hydrolysis and dehydration of chitosan/polymerization of acrylamide took place, finally high yield CDs were obtained. Therefore, the two-component carbon sources can react with each other to achieve high yield of CDs. Under the influence of the microwave irradiation, the grafting reaction between acrylamide and chitosan was initiated due to the generation of free radicals. At the same time, acrylamide is polymerized to form polyacrylamide under microwave irradiation. Chitosan possessed large numbers of pendent -NH2 and -OH groups and provided probable sites for grafting reactions. Microwave irradiations lowered the Gibb's free energy of activation of the reactions and create free radicals [39,40], this allowed chitosan to be grafted with polyacrylamide to form chain polymers. We hypothesized that grafting polymers served as intermediate products for synthesis of CDs in microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. Actually, grafting reaction between acrylamide and chitosan by microwave irradiations was reported previously. In the grafting reaction, hydroxyl groups of chitosan reacted with acrylamide, and acrylamide was covalently linkages on the C3 hydroxyl group of chitosan [40]. Amino groups of chitosan can also react with acrylamide [39]. Based on the literatures of grafting reaction between acrylamide and chitosan, chitosan can be grafted with acrylamide through the generation of the free radicals under the influence of the microwaves. Therefore, we proposed that grafting polymers served as intermediate product for synthesis of CDs under microwave-hydrothermal carbonization (Fig. 1A), which can further improve the synthetic yield of CDs.

The synthetic yield of CDs-NH2 was higher than CDs derived from chitosan or acrylamide alone. The synthetic yield of CDs derived from chitosan and acrylamide were as low as 6.7% and 8.3%, respectively (Fig. 1B). The synthetic yield of CDs (45.9%) was improved 5.85 times higher than CDs derived from chitosan. When the content of carbon‑carbon double bonds (CC content) was increased from 0 mmol to 56 mmol, the synthetic yield increasing 6.7% to 45.9%, which exhibiting a 5.85-fold increases. To verify the universality of carbon-carbon double bonds boosting high-yield synthesis, different precursor containing carbon-carbon double bonds (AE, BA, AA and AAM) were used as precursor to synthesize CDs. We observed improvement the synthetic yield of CDs derived from AE, BA, AA and AAM (18.5%, 20.7%, 25.9% and 45.9%) as shown in Fig. 1C. In Fig. 1D, the synthetic yield of CDs derived from graphite oxide, chitosan, acrylamide, acrylamide/chitosan were 2.5%, 6.7%, 8.3%, and 45.9%, respectively. In Table 1 , different materials served as precursors for synthesis of CDs via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization, however few of literatures reported the synthetic yield of CDs, expect that 2.5% derived from graphite oxide. The synthetic yield of carbon dots derived from chitosan and acrylamide were as low as 6.7% and 8.3%, respectively. It is challenge for synthesis carbon dots with high synthetic yield (>10%) via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization. The synthetic yield of CDs (45.9%) derived from acrylamide/chitosan was improved 5.85 and 4.53 times higher than CDs derived from chitosan or acrylamide, respectively. Indeed, the synthetic yield (45.9%) of CDs-NH2 is highest compared with that reported in literatures via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and surface properties of carbon dots with amino groups and carboxylic group
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optical and surface properties of CDs-NH2, we characterized the PL, FTIR and XRD. The PL emission wavelength shifted to longer wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength from 300 to 400 nm. The strongest fluorescence emission spectrum was observed with a peak at 409 nm when using 330 nm excitation wavelengths, enabling the observation of visible color shift from blue to green (Fig. 2A). CDs-NH2 displayed a characteristic excitation-dependent PL behavior, which was the specific evidence for CDs. The PL quantum yield (QY) for the as-prepared CDs was 12.17%, compared with the fluorescent CDs of chitosan as a single carbon source, the quantum yield of CDs-NH2 increased by 103%. In Fig. 2B, the emission fluorescence of CDs-COOH was also shown a dependence on the excitation wavelength. The maximum excitation wavelength was 360 nm and the maximum emission wavelength was 448 nm.

Spectra of CDs-NH2 and CDs-COOH are very similar in Fig. 2C and D. In Fig. 2C, the absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 was assigned to the carboxylic groups and amino groups of chitosan and polyacrylamide. The absorption peaks at 1660, 1450, 1193 and 1119 cm−1 were characteristic peaks of polyacrylamide. The absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 was derived from amide I band in polyacrylamide, and the absorption peak at 1450 cm−1 was derived from methylene deformation. The absorption peaks of 1193 and 1119 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of CN. The absorption peaks at 1720, 1547, 1402, 1165 and 1066 cm−1 were characteristic peaks of chitosan. The absorption peak at 1720 cm−1 was carbonyl groups of chitosan. The peak at 1547 cm−1 corresponded to amino groups in chitosan. The absorption peak at 1402 cm−1 corresponded to –CH2 bending mode. 1165 cm−1 belonged to characteristic peak of chitosan. The absorption peak at 1066 cm−1 was CO stretching of saccharide structure. The amino groups and carboxylic groups in CDs-NH2 contributed by polyacrylamide and chitosan. In Fig. 2D, absorption peaks at 2958, 1450, 1408 and 1040 cm−1 were assigned to chitosan. The absorption peak at 2958 cm−1 was CH stretching mode original from –CH2. At 1450 and 1408 cm−1, the two peaks were –CH2 bending mode. The absorption peak of 1040 cm−1 was CO stretching mode of CH2-OH of saccharide structure. Absorption peaks at 1709, 1246 and 1174 cm−1 were assigned to polyacrylic acid. The absorption peak at 1709 cm−1 was assigned to the vibrations of CO of the carboxylic group. The bands at 1246 and 1174 cm−1 were assigned to –OH bending vibrations and –CO stretching. Carboxylic groups in CDs-COOH mainly contributed by polyacrylic acid.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs-NH2 is hardly changed in the range of pH 4–10 (Fig. 2E), and the fluorescence intensity fluctuation value is <2.1%. Even under the strong alkaline condition (pH = 12), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained high, about 89% of the neutral condition, which paved the way for the subsequent stable detection of metal ions. As shown in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensity of CDs with carboxyl group was almost unchanged in the range of pH 2–12. The fluorescence intensity fluctuation value was <1.8%. Under strong acidity and alkalinity conditions, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remained high, about 99% of the neutral conditions. To further illustrate the structural of the CDs-NH2 and CDs-COOH, XRD analysis was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 2G, the peak located at 21° was observed in the XRD pattern of CDs-NH2. For CDs-COOH, only a broad diffraction peak at 21° was observed. The above results suggested that as-prepared CDs-NH2 and CDs-COOH were small amorphous nanoparticles.

The black column in Fig. 3A showed the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution before adding Na2EDTA, and the red column showed the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution after the adding of strong metal chelating agent (Na2EDTA). The results showed that the fluorescence intensities of the mixed solutions were hardly changed. Na2EDTA cannot chelate with Fe2+ from CDs-NH2, which indicated that CDs-NH2 posesses potential ability as a powerful metal chelating agent.

The XPS measurements provided more information on the surface structures of CDs-NH2. Fig. 3B, C, D showed the XPS survey spectra of CDs-NH2. The XPS spectrum of N1s core electrons exhibited two fitted peaks at 399.8 and 401.4 eV, which were associated with N in a graphite-like structure (NC3), pyridinic-like N, and NH bond of NH2 groups, respectively. Their relative contents were ca. 86% and 14% respectively on the basis of calculations of the integral area as marked in Fig. 3B. The C1s XPS spectrum can be divided into three different peaks corresponding to different C species. The main peak at 284.8 eV originated from sp2-hybridized (C=C) and sp3-hybridized (CC) carbon and the peaks at 285.4and 288.4 eV were attributed to CO, -C=O, and CN bonds, respectively. Meanwhile, the O1s XPS spectrum can be divided into two different peaks locating at 531.6 and 533.1 eV, which originated from OC, and OC, respectively. The XPS analysis indicated that the relative content of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs-NH2 was high, which was consistent with the FTIR spectroscopy results. The abundant amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs-NH2 may be the reason why the chelating ability of CDs-NH2 was better than EDTA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Fe2+ detection using CDs-NH2 as fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the selectivity and specificity of CDs-NH2 as a fluorescent probe for ferrous ions, PL intensities were analyzed in the presence of different metal ions at a concentration of 300.0 μM. Fig. 4A was the fluorescence spectrum of 11 kinds of metal ions mixed with CDs-NH2 solution and 0.2 mg/ml CDs-NH2 original solution. The PL intensity of CDs-NH2 was strongly quenched (55% attenuation) in the case of Fe2+. Based on this phenomenon, CDs-NH2 offered potential to selectively detect Fe2+ in the presence of other metal ions at concentrations of 300 μM. Pb2+ and Mn2+ enhanced its fluorescence of the CDs-NH2, while Co2+, Ni2+, Sn4+ and Nd3+ had little effect on the fluorescence of CDs-NH2. Cr3+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ had different degree of quenching effect on the fluorescence effect of CDs-NH2, but their fluorescence quenching did not exceed 30%. When the addition of Fe2+ reduced the fluorescence intensity by nearly 55%, and the quenching effect of CDs-NH2 was significant. From Fig. 4B, even if the different metal ions had different quenching effect on CDs-NH2, the result revealed that there was little interference from the various metal ions. The difference of relative fluorescence intensity between the mixed solution before and after adding Fe2+ was about 50%, and the quenching effect of Fe2+ was obvious. The interference of Sn4+ was the strongest, the interference of Cu2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+ were the weakest, but the difference of fluorescence intensity was not >0.15, which indicated that the anti-interference ability of CDs-NH2 in detecting Fe2+ was good and it is of great significance to detect Fe2+ in real water sample. In case of water containing ferric ions or ferrous ions, the carbon dots with chelating ability can detection ferric ions and ferrous ions, respectively. The CDs-NH2 can detect these two species of metal ions, and the effect of detecting ferrous ions was better than that of ferric ions. When ferrous ions and ferric ions were mixed together, CDs-NH2 cannot distinguish them. In case of water containing ferric ions, the indirect detection method can be adopted. For example, the ferric ions can be converted into ferrous ions by designed reduction reaction between sodium sulfite and ferric ions. The total ferrous ions were tested by manuscript stated method. To calculate the concentration of Fe3+, the total value molar of ferrous ion subtracted the consumed molar of sodium sulfite. So we can calculate not only the concentration of Fe2+ but also the concentration of Fe3+.

For sensitivity tests, PL intensity upon adding different concentrations of Fe2+ ranging from 0 to 300 μM was monitored. As shown in Fig. 4C, the PL intensity of CDs-NH2 showed a gradual decrease with increasing Fe2+ concentration, revealing that sensing was sensitive to Fe2+. Two good linear relationships (0–50 and 100–300 μM) existed between PL intensity and Fe2+ concentration in Fig. 4D. At low concentrations (Fig. 4D) it fits the linear Stern-Volmer equation, illustrating a photophysical process by a single reaction. The detection limit was as low as 160 nM at signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, which was sensitive enough to alert the ferrous ion in drinking water. The threshold concentration of ferrous ions in drinking water is 1.78 μM (1780 nM) according to world health organization guidelines for drinking-water quality. Different precursors had been used to synthesize CDs for Fe2+ detection as shown in Table 2 . CDs derived from succinic acid and glycerol shown the limits of detection of 21,900 nM of Fe2+ [41]. CDs derived from cornstalk exhibited the linear in the concentration range of 0–18 μM and 180 nM detection limit for Fe2+ [42]. CDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine possessed the detection limit of 170 nM and the linear range of 100–500 μM for Fe2+ according to indirectly measurement the content of Fe2+ by determining Fe3+ [43]. The detection limit of the CDs-NH2 was most sensitive among of literatures (Table 2), because CDs-NH2 with chelating ability had strong ability to detect Fe2+. Therefore, CDs-NH2 with chelating ability can report ferrous ions sensitively. The CDs-NH2 synthesized by microwave-hydrothermal carbonization had wide linear concentration range and low detection limit. The reason for the chelation of CDs with metal ions was attributed to the existence of abundant -NH2 and -COOH groups on the surface of CDs-NH2 which can bind metal ions. As shown in Fig. 4E, the -NH2 and -COOH of CDs-NH2 shown the behavior of chelating ability with metal ions. The binding with metal ions will change the surface energy level states of CDs-NH2, which will change the trajectory of electron transition from ground state to excited state or the way of electron return to ground state after transition. Abundant amino groups and carboxylic groups of CDs-NH2 will coordinate with Fe2+. The complex promotes charge transferring and thus inhibits exciton recombination, leading to significant fluorescence quenching. Miao et al. [17] indicated that the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots by Fe3+ was not only due to the coordination of carboxyl groups of carbon dots with Fe3+, but also due to the interaction of other functional groups with Fe3+. The CDs-NH2 with chelating ability mainly provided amino groups and carboxylic groups to bind ferrous ions, which formed CDs-NH2/Fe2+ complex accompany with florescence quenching.

High-yield synthesis of CDs-NH2 (45.9%) derived from acrylamide/chitosan was synthesized via microwave-hydrothermal carbonization in 30–60 min. The synthetic yield of CDs-NH2 was improved 5.85 and 4.53 times higher than carbon dots derived from chitosan or acrylamide, respectively. Chitosan grafted poly-acrylamide served as precursor for carbon dots during microwave-hydrothermal carbonization, which boosted the synthetic yield of carbon dots. CDs-NH2 were shown stronger chelating ability to ferrous ions than EDTA due the presence of amino groups and carboxylic groups. CDs-NH2 with chelating ability can report the ferrous ions with linear concentration range of 0–50 μM and detection limit of 160 nM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Science Foundation of China (51372051, 51621091), State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment of Harbin Institute of Technology (2019TS01).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A non-haem iron centre in the transcription factor NorR senses nitric oxide
